season.comparisonNote: Season running 18% above the multi-year average. The overnight flight pushes the season total to its highest point so far.

migration.narrativeSummary: Heavy migration last night — 920K birds aloft — ran straight into a rain band that swept through SW Ohio between midnight and 4 AM. Classic fallout conditions: birds were forced down mid-flight by the precipitation. The rain cleared before dawn, and right now there are almost certainly dense concentrations of grounded migrants in wooded parks across Hamilton County. Get out NOW.

today.migrationInterpretation: Textbook fallout morning. Rain stopped at 4:30 AM, skies clearing rapidly, NW wind 18 mph. Dense concentrations of exhausted migrants should be visible in any wooded shelter.
today.frontalNote: Cold front passed 4:30 AM — rain stopped, winds shifted NW. Heavy overnight rain (70% precip) grounded migrants mid-flight. Conditions now clearing rapidly. This is a fallout event — birds concentrated in any wooded or shrubby cover, often in numbers 5-10× normal. Act now: this window closes by mid-morning as birds resume movement.

outlook.birdingWindow: FALLOUT — get out immediately, window closing mid-morning

birdingWindow.note: Arrive by 5:47 AM for peak dawn chorus. Fallout window closes ~10 AM as birds resume flight.

listservSightings.subject: Fallout at Otto Armleder — unreal this morning
listservSightings.body: Got there at 6 AM and immediately knew something was different. Every shrub had birds. Connecticut Warblers (2!), Bay-breasted Warblers everywhere, Cape May Warblers in the treetops, Blackpoll Warblers calling from every direction. Counted 28 warbler species in 2 hours. This is the fallout we've been waiting for. The thicket along the mud trail to the bean field had 50+ birds packed into 100 feet of shrubs. Get there NOW.

listservSightings.subject: Shawnee Lookout — dense fallout, multiple rare birds
listservSightings.body: Shawnee is absolutely loaded. Yellow-bellied Flycatcher, Olive-sided Flycatcher (rare!), multiple Connecticut Warblers, Cape May Warblers, Blackpoll everywhere. Every trail is productive. The rain last night stopped everything in its tracks.

L298164.name: Otto Armleder Memorial Park
L298164.habitatSummary: Riparian cottonwood forest, shrubby thickets, agricultural edge. During fallout events, the wooded areas and shrub edge concentrate extraordinary numbers of migrants.

trails.name: Paved loop / mud trail to bean field
trails.habitat: Dense shrubby thicket, overgrown field edge, wet tangles
trails.directions: From the main parking lot, take the paved trail east. At the junction where pavement ends and a muddy path continues toward the bean field, check the dense shrubby thicket on both sides. During fallout events, this 100-foot stretch can hold dozens of warblers packed into the shrubs.
trails.bestTime: First 3 hours of daylight during fallout events
trails.notes: During fallout conditions, walk slowly and look into every shrub at eye level. Birds will be exhausted and easy to approach.
